segments.purgatorio-17-g68: This segment is only the closing poetic quotation mark and punctuation that ends the previously quoted speech; it has no lexical content to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-17-g69: 'Già' is an adverb meaning 'by then/already' placed before the verb for emphasis. 'Eran' is the archaic poetic form of 'erano' (3rd pl. imperfect of essere) used with the past participle 'levati' to form a compound verbal idea meaning 'had risen/ were raised'; Dante puts the subject 'li ultimi raggi' after the verb (inversion) for metrical/poetic effect. 'Sovra noi' is a prepositional phrase 'above us', and 'tanto' modifies 'levati' to indicate degree ('so much/so high'). The relative clause 'che la notte segue' ('that/which follows the night') postmodifies 'raggi' and uses present tense 'segue' as a timeless or habitual characteristic of those rays.
segments.purgatorio-17-g70: The conjunction 'che' introduces the result clause ('so... that') triggered by 'tanto' in the previous segment. 'Le stelle' is the subject and 'apparivan' is the imperfect (3rd pl.) meaning 'were appearing' or 'appeared' in a repeated/ongoing sense; the -van ending is a poetic imperfect form. 'Da più lati' is a locative phrase 'from many sides/directions', with 'più' meaning 'several/many'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g71: This is a vocative exclamation: 'O' (vocative particle) addresses 'virtù mia' directly. 'Virtù' here is used as 'power/strength' or 'virtue' and 'mia' follows the noun as the possessive adjective 'my', a normal placement in Italian poetry.
segments.purgatorio-17-g72: 'Perché' is the interrogative 'why' introducing the question. 'Sì' is an adverb meaning 'so/thus' intensifying the verb. 'Ti' is the clitic reflexive/object pronoun 'you/yourself' referring back to 'virtù', and 'dilegue' is the 3rd-person singular present form (poetic spelling with -e) of 'dileguare' meaning 'vanish/dissipate'; together 'perché sì ti dilegue?' = 'why do you vanish so?'
segments.purgatorio-17-g73: This segment is punctuation (a quotation mark and comma) marking the end of the inner speech and the return to the narrator's voice; it carries no lexical meaning.
segments.purgatorio-17-g74: 'Fra me stesso' is an idiomatic reflexive expression meaning 'to myself' or 'within myself' (literally 'among myself'). 'Dicea' is the imperfect 1st-person singular (poetic form of 'dicevo') meaning 'I said' or 'I was saying', indicating internal reflection; the comma closes the parenthetical remark.
segments.purgatorio-17-g75: 'Ché' (because/for) introduces the explanatory clause. 'Mi' is the indirect object 'to me'; 'sentiva' is the imperfect of 'sentire' meaning 'I felt' (a continuing sensation). 'La possa' uses an older feminine noun form for 'power/strength' (so 'la possa' = 'the power'); 'de' is the archaic 'di' ('of'); 'le gambe' 'the legs'; 'posta' is the past participle of 'porre' ('placed') agreeing with the feminine noun 'possa'; 'in triegue' ('in truce') is a figurative locution meaning the strength was put into a suspension or respite. The whole phrase means 'for I felt the power of my legs put into truce' (i.e. my legs had ceased to work normally).
segments.purgatorio-17-g36: Io son = “I am,” with son as the contracted present of essere used for the first person; essa is a feminine pronoun identifying the speaker (“she”). che introduces a relative clause modifying essa; lutto is a noun meaning mourning/grief and here completes the relative clause (“who mourns”).
segments.purgatorio-17-g37: madre is direct address (vocative) meaning “mother”; it stands alone as a brief, emphatic interjection to the addressee. Word order and punctuation mark it off from the surrounding clause.
segments.purgatorio-17-g38: a la tua pria … ch’a l’altrui ruina is a comparative/priority construction: a la tua pria = “for you first” (a + definite article + tua + pria = “for/at your (sake) before”), ch’ (che) introduces the comparison/contrast, and a l’altrui ruina = “at/for another’s ruin.” The whole phrase means the speaker grieves for the addressee before (rather than for) someone else’s downfall.
segments.purgatorio-17-g39: Come introduces a simile (“as/just as”); si frange is a reflexive/passive construction in the present used generically (“is broken/splintered”); il sonno is the subject. ove = “where,” and di butto is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning “suddenly/all at once” modifying the action; nova luce percuote il viso chiuso = “a new light strikes the closed face,” with percuote (present) as the verb and chiuso (past participle/adjective) following viso.
segments.purgatorio-17-g40: che (= which/that) resumes the preceding clause and describes the effect: fratto (a past participle meaning “rent/broken”) + guizza (present “flashes/darts”) — the face flashes — pria che introduces a temporal clause “before” and is followed by the subjunctive muoia (“may die/perish”), with tutto (“completely/entirely”) qualifying the dying.
segments.purgatorio-17-g41: così = “thus/so”; l’imaginar mio (the poetic form of ‘l’immaginare’ used as a noun) = “my imagination.” cadde is passato remoto (“fell”); giuso is an adverb “down” (here archaic/poetic). tosto che = “as soon as,” and lume il volto mi percosse = “light struck my face,” percosse being passato remoto of percuotere with mi marking the object (“struck me in the face”).
segments.purgatorio-17-g42: maggior assai = “much greater/more”; che here functions as a comparative “than.” quel ch’è in nostro uso = “that which is in our custom/what is usual for us,” with ch’ (= che) introducing the relative clause and in nostro uso meaning “in our custom/practice.”
segments.purgatorio-17-g43: I’ (poetic io) mi volgea is an imperfect reflexive of volgere (“I was turning myself/turned”), expressing an ongoing past action; per veder = “in order to see” (per + infinitive expresses purpose); ov’ io fosse uses an archaic subjunctive (fosse) in an indirect/free clause meaning “where I was” or “where I might be.”
segments.purgatorio-17-g44: quando = “when”; una voce disse = “a voice said” (past); the quoted phrase Qui si monta uses an impersonal reflexive si + monta = “one climbs/people climb” or “here you go up,” with Qui marking the place.
segments.purgatorio-17-g45: che = “which” (relative) referring to the voice’s remark; da ogne altro intento = “from every other intention/purpose” (da + ogne = away from every); mi rimosse is passato remoto of rimuovere (“it removed/distracted me”).
segments.purgatorio-17-g46: e fece = “and it made” (fece = past of fare); la mia voglia tanto pronta = “my desire so ready/eager”; di riguardar = “to look at/regard” (infinitive introduced by di); chi era che parlava = “who it was that was speaking,” a nested relative/identifying clause with era and parlava in the imperfect backgrounding the action.
segments.purgatorio-17-g47: che = relative “that”; mai non posa = “never rests/ceases” (mai non + present verb for habitual/general truth); posa here means “rest/cease” rather than “place.”
segments.purgatorio-17-g48: se non = “unless” (literally “if not”); si raffronta is reflexive present meaning “is/gets compared” or more idiomatically “is brought face to face/encountered,” so the clause says the desire does not rest unless it is thus satisfied/confronted.
segments.purgatorio-17-g49: Ma come = “but as/just as” introducing the comparison; al sol here means “to/by the sun” (poetic/elliptical usage). che nostra vista grava = “which burdens/oppresses our sight” (che relative; grava = present indicative ‘weighs down’). e per soverchio sua figura vela = “and by its excess it veils (our sight) with its form/appearance,” where per soverchio = “by/exceedingly” and vela = “veils/obscures.”
segments.purgatorio-17-g50: così = “thus/so”; la mia virtù = “my power/strength/faculty”; quivi = archaic adverb “there”; mancava is imperfect of mancare meaning “was lacking/fail(ed)”: the speaker’s power failed there (in that moment/place).
segments.purgatorio-17-g147: L’ is the elided definite article (il) before a vowel. Amor is the masculine noun 'love' as the sentence subject. ch’ (che) is the relative pronoun 'that/which' introducing the relative clause. Ad esso means 'to it' (esso referring back to something in the prior discourse). Troppo modifies the verb and means 'too much'. S’ is the reflexive clitic (si) and abbandona is 3rd person singular present of abbandonare; together s’abbandona (abbandonarsi) means 'to give oneself up / surrender oneself', so the clause literally says 'the love that surrenders itself too much to it.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g148: Di sovr’ a noi is an archaic/poetic order for 'di sovra a noi' meaning 'from over/above us' (sovr' = sovra). Si piange uses the reflexive/impersonal si to form an impersonal or passive idea ('one laments' / 'there is lamentation' / 'it is lamented'). Per introduces the extent or medium and with cerchi here means 'in/through three circles.' Note: 'cerchi' is the plural of cerchio 'circles' (the provided senses list did not include this meaning).
segments.purgatorio-17-g149: Ma is the adversative 'but'. Come functions as 'as' / 'in the way that'. Tripartito is an adjective meaning 'three-part' or 'threefold' (literally 'divided into three'). Si ragiona uses the impersonal/passive si with ragionare: here it means 'it is spoken of / it is treated / one reasons', so the sense is 'it is discussed/treated as threefold.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g150: Tacciolo is a compacted/colloquial poetic form meaning roughly 'I keep silent about it' or 'I will say no more about it.' Grammatically it functions as a first-person declaration — essentially taccio + lo ('I silence it' / 'I hold it silent'), used here as a brief interjection signalling the speaker will stop commenting.
segments.purgatorio-17-g151: Acciò (archaic) = 'affinché' = 'so that' / 'in order that' and introduces a purpose clause, hence the following verb appears in the subjunctive mood. Che is the subordinating particle 'that'. Tu is the subject 'you'. Per te 'for yourself' / 'by yourself' (emphatic reflexive sense: 'on your own account'). Ne is the clitic pronoun 'of it' / 'about it' and appears before the verb (standard clitic placement). Cerchi is 2nd person singular present subjunctive (form identical to the indicative) of cercare here used in a purposive clause: 'that you may seek of it / seek it for yourself.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g1: Ricorditi is an imperative addressed to the second person singular with the enclitic reflexive pronoun -ti attached (modern Italian ricordati). It commands the reader to 'remember' and stands alone as a vocative/imperative opening.
segments.purgatorio-17-g2: Lettor is the vocative form of 'reader' (archaic spelling of lettore). It's an address to the person being spoken to and functions independently of the sentence's verbal inflections.
segments.purgatorio-17-g3: This whole clause is a conditional protasis introduced by se mai (= 'if ever'). Ne l’alpe is an older split form meaning 'in the Alp/on the mountain' (ne = in + l' = the). Ti colse uses colse (passato remoto of cogliere in the sense 'seize, overtake') with the clitic ti as direct object: 'a fog seized/overtook you.' Per la qual introduces a relative clause 'by/through which'; vedessi is an imperfect subjunctive used in a concessive/result clause meaning 'you could see' or 'you would see.' Non altrimenti che ... is a negative comparative: 'no otherwise than through the skin of a mole.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g4: Come here is a conjunction meaning 'as' or 'how' and introduces the illustrative example that follows; it links the previous conditional to the description to come.
segments.purgatorio-17-g5: Quando = 'when.' I vapori umidi e spessi is the subject ('the moist and dense vapors'); a + infinitive (a diradar) is a periphrastic marker meaning 'to begin to thin,' and cominciansi is the archaic 3rd-person plural present (reflecting the subject 'vapori') meaning 'they begin' (note the -si reflexive ending common in older Italian for intransitive verbs).
segments.purgatorio-17-g6: La spera is an older spelling for 'la sfera' (the sphere). Del sol = 'of the sun' (here the celestial sphere or disc of the sun). Debilemente enters as an adverb 'weakly, feebly'; entra per essi: 'enters through them' (essi = the vapors). The clause describes sunlight penetrating the thinning vapors.
segments.purgatorio-17-g7: E fia is future (subjunctive/poetic) of essere used as 'and will be' or 'and will become.' La tua imagine leggera = 'your light image/mental picture' (imagine = imaginative visual impression, leggera = quick/light). In giugnere a veder: poetic infinitive phrase meaning 'quick in reaching to see' (giugnere = to reach), and com' io rividi = 'how I saw again' (rividi = I saw again) — the clause predicts the reader's imagination will quickly reach to see what the poet again saw.
segments.purgatorio-17-g8: Che introduces a relative clause modifying 'lo sole' (the sun): 'which already was nel corcar.' Già = 'already.' Nel corcar era uses corcar (to lie down) poetically for 'to set' (of the sun); era is the imperfect 'was' — 'which was already setting.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g9: Sì here is a connective adverb meaning 'so' or 'thus,' linking this action to what was just described; it can also emphasize the affirmation of what follows.
segments.purgatorio-17-g10: Pareggiando is a present gerund meaning 'matching' or 'keeping equal with.' I miei co’ passi fidi is an older phrasing: co’ is a contraction of con + article ('with the'), passi fidi = 'faithful steps' (fidi = faithful/trusting) of del mio maestro = 'of my master.' The whole phrase is circumstantial: 'matching my steps with the faithful steps of my master.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g11: Usci' fuor (uscì fuor) is a past tense ('I/we/they went out' in older spelling; context shows the pilgrim leaving the cloud). Di tal nube = 'from such a cloud.' Ai raggi morti già ne’ bassi lidi: ai = 'into/to the,' raggi morti literally 'dead rays' (poetically 'waning or faint rays' as the sun nears setting), già = 'already,' ne’ bassi lidi = 'on the low shores' (ne' = in the/at the + lidi = shores).
segments.purgatorio-17-g12: O is an exclamation 'O' or 'oh' addressing the faculty named. Imaginativa is a substantive meaning the imaginative faculty or imagination. Che here is a relative pronoun 'that/which.' Ne rube talvolta = 'steals from it/from us sometimes' (rube = steals); talvolta = 'sometimes'; sì di fuor = 'so / to such an extent outwardly' (di fuor = outwards). The clause reproaches imagination for wandering outward.
segments.purgatorio-17-g13: Ch' om non s’accorge is a result clause: 'that one does not notice.' Ch' = che 'that' used to introduce the consequence; om = 'one/man'; non s’accorge = 'doesn't notice' (s' = reflexive clitic, accorge = accorgere 'to notice/realize'). Perché dintorno suonin mille tube = 'because all around a thousand trumpets sound' (perché = 'because' giving the reason the man does not notice the imagination leaving).
segments.purgatorio-17-g14: Chi move te, is a direct question: chi = 'who' (or 'what') + move = present 'moves/prompts' + te = 'you' (accusative). Dante asks what causes/sets the imagination in motion.
segments.purgatorio-17-g15: Se 'l senso non ti porge? is a conditional question: se = 'if'; 'l senso = 'the sense' (i.e., perception or the sensible input); non ti porge = 'does not offer/supply you' (porgere used transitively: to present or afford). The question asks whether anything moves the imagination aside from what the senses furnish.
segments.purgatorio-17-g16: Moveti is an imperative with enclitic -ti ('move yourself' or 'stir yourself'); the verb is addressed to lume ('light'). Che nel ciel s’informa is a relative clause: nel ciel = 'in heaven/sky' and s'informa (s' + informa) uses informare in the sense 'to take form/be shaped' (the reflexive indicating the light is formed/takes shape in the heavens).
segments.purgatorio-17-g17: Per sé o per voler che giù lo scorge gives two possible agents: per sé = 'by itself' (i.e., inherently), or per voler = 'by the will' (meaning God's will or a willing cause). Che introduces a subordinate clause modifying 'voler': giù = 'below/down here'; lo scorge = 'discerns/perceives it' (scorgere = to perceive/see), so: 'whether by itself or by the will that below perceives it.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g18: This clause begins with the partitive/agent phrase 'De l’empiezza di lei' ('From the impiety of her'), where 'l’' is the definite article before the abstract noun 'empiezza' (impiety). The relative clause 'che mutò forma ne l’uccel' uses the passato remoto 'mutò' (he/she/it changed) to describe a completed transformation; 'ne' here means 'into' + 'l’uccel' ('the bird'). The infinitival phrase 'ch’a cantar più si diletta' has the preverbal particle 'a' marking purpose or the infinitival construction ('to sing'), 'si' is the reflexive pronoun in the pronominal verb 'si diletta' = 'takes pleasure', and 'più' modifies the comparative 'most'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g19: 'Ne l’imagine mia apparve l’orma' literally places the adverbial 'ne' (= in) before 'l’imagine mia' ('my imagination/mental image'), then the verb in passato remoto 'apparve' ('appeared'). 'L’orma' is the subject that appears; here 'orma' means an imprint or trace left by the transformed creature.
segments.purgatorio-17-g20: This is a copular clause: 'e qui fu la mia mente sì ristretta dentro da sé'. 'Fu' is passato remoto of 'essere' ('was'); 'sì ristretta' is a participial adjective ('so narrowed/contracted'); 'dentro da sé' is a poetic way to say 'within itself/herself' indicating inward withdrawal of the mind.
segments.purgatorio-17-g21: This relative result clause begins with 'che' introducing what follows: 'di fuor non venìa cosa che fosse allor da lei ricetta.' 'Di fuor' = 'from outside'; 'venìa' is the imperfect of 'venire' ('came'/'would come'), used here with negative 'non' to indicate nothing from outside came in. The subordinate clause uses the imperfect subjunctive 'fosse' after 'cosa che' to express a non-actualized or evaluated circumstance; 'ricetta' is a past participle meaning 'received' or 'accepted' (feminine form to agree with 'cosa' or implied subject).
segments.purgatorio-17-g22: 'Poi piovve dentro a l’alta fantasia un crucifisso' uses the pictorial verb 'piovve' (passato remoto of 'piovere' literally 'it rained') metaphorically to mean 'fell' or 'came down' into the speaker's mind. 'Dentro a l’alta fantasia' ('into the lofty imagination') places the location; 'un crucifisso' is the emergent object.
segments.purgatorio-17-g23: 'Dispettoso e fero ne la sua vista' are two adjectives (dispettoso, fero) describing the crucifix; 'ne la sua vista' means 'in its sight/appearance' or 'as it was seen', with 'ne' = 'in' and 'vista' = 'appearance' or 'sight'. The whole phrase characterizes the vision as spiteful and fierce to the observer's eyes.
segments.purgatorio-17-g24: 'e cotal si moria;' is an elliptical comment: 'e' = 'and', 'cotal' = 'such/so', 'si moria' is an impersonal/semi-reflexive imperfect meaning 'it died away' or 'it seemed to die.' The combination gives the sense 'and it so withered/died away' (a poetic descriptive action).
segments.purgatorio-17-g25: 'intorno ad esso era il grande Assüero' is a simple locative clause: 'intorno ad esso' = 'around it', 'era' (passato remoto/imperfect here rendered 'stood'/'was present') and the proper name 'Assüero' (Ahasuerus) is the subject complement introduced with the definite article 'il'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g26: This is a noun list identifying those present: 'Estèr sua sposa e ’l giusto Mardoceo'. 'sua sposa' = 'his wife' modifies the name Esther. Note: the lemma/sense given for 'Estèr' in the data is not the proper-name sense; here it is the proper name 'Esther' and should be treated as such.
segments.purgatorio-17-g27: 'che fu al dire e al far così intero' is a relative clause modifying Mardoceo: 'che' = 'who', 'fu' = past tense 'was', and the idiom 'al dire e al far' ('in speech and in action') describes the domains in which he was 'intero' — here used in the moral sense 'upright/whole' (that is, consistent and blameless in both word and deed).
segments.purgatorio-17-g28: 'E come questa imagine rompeo sé per sé stessa' uses 'come' = 'as' to introduce a simile; 'rompeo' is passato remoto first/third-person form used poetically for 'broke' (here impersonal/visual narration), and the doubled reflexives 'sé per sé stessa' intensify that the image broke itself 'of itself/by itself' (poetic emphatic construction).
segments.purgatorio-17-g29: The simile 'a guisa d’una bulla cui manca l’acqua sotto qual si feo' compares the breaking image to a 'bulla' (bubble) whose supporting water beneath is missing. 'Cui manca' = 'to which there is lacking' and 'sotto qual si feo' ('beneath which it was made') uses 'feo' (poetic past of 'fare') meaning 'was made'—an explanatory relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-17-g30: 'surse in mia visïone una fanciulla piangendo forte' has the intransitive passato remoto 'surse' ('arose/appeared') as the main verb; 'in mia visione' gives the locus, 'una fanciulla' is the subject that appears, and the participial phrase 'piangendo forte' ('weeping loudly') describes the manner of her appearance.
segments.purgatorio-17-g31: The simple connector 'e' ('and') links the appearance to speech: 'dicea' is the imperfect 'was saying' used to report ongoing speech in the past; it prepares the quoted utterance that follows.
segments.purgatorio-17-g32: The vocative '«O regina,' is the direct address. 'O' is the interjection 'oh', here calling out to a queen. Note: the lexical entry given for 'regina' in the data is anomalous; in the text it is the noun 'queen'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g33: A direct question: 'perché per ira hai voluto esser nulla?' 'Perché' = 'why', 'per ira' = 'by/through anger', and 'hai voluto' is the present perfect of 'volere' ('you have wished/chosen'), followed by the infinitive 'esser' and the predicate 'nulla' ('nothing'). The construction expresses reproach: 'why did you choose through anger to become nothing?'
segments.purgatorio-17-g34: 'Ancisa t’hai per non perder Lavina' places the past participle/adjectival 'Ancisa' (here functioning as 'you have killed/cut yourself'—a poetic passive/participial construction) before the clitic subject 't’' and the auxiliary 'hai' forming a perfect tense. 'Per non perder Lavina' gives the purpose/reason 'so as not to lose Lavinia.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g35: 'or m’hai perduta!' is an exclamatory result: 'or' = 'now', 'm’' is the clitic for 'me' (direct object, here marked in the data with an irregular lemma), 'hai perduta' = 'you have lost (me)' with the past participle 'perduta' agreeing in gender with the (implied) feminine direct object.
segments.purgatorio-17-g133: Questo ('this') modifies the following nominal phrase; triforme is an adjective formed from Latin/Italian meaning 'three‑formed' placed before the noun for emphasis. Amor is the noun 'love.' Qua giù di sotto is a stack of local adverbials ('here', 'down', 'below') reinforcing location. Si piange is a reflexive/impersonal construction: si + verb (piangere) yields 'is lamented' or 'people lament it' (passive/impersonal sense) rather than a literal 'he/she weeps.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g134: Or vo' is an archaic/poetic phrase: or = 'now'; vo' is a contracted form of voglio ('I want/I will') and introduces a purpose/exhortation. Che introduces the subordinate clause ('that'); tu is the subject 'you.' De l'altro = 'of/about the other' (de = di used before the elided article l'). Intende is present indicative of intendere meaning 'understand' here ('grasp' or 'take note of').
segments.purgatorio-17-g135: Che here is a relative pronoun 'which/that' referring back to 'the other.' Corre is present indicative of correre used metaphorically ('runs, hastens'); al = a + il 'to the.' Ben (short for 'bene' used as a substantive) means 'the good' (the object of desire). Con ordine corrotto is a prepositional phrase 'with order corrupted' — ordine is the abstract noun 'order' and corrotto is a past participle/adjective meaning 'perverted/corrupt'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g136: Ciascun = 'each one' (subject). Confusamente is an adverb modifying apprende ('confusedly/indistinctly'). Un bene is the indefinite phrase 'a good' (some good/benefit). Apprende is present indicative of apprendere here in the sense 'takes hold of, grasps' (intellectually or as an object of desire). Nel qual ('in which') introduces a relative clause; si queti l'animo uses si as reflexive/impersonal with the transitive verb quetare (to calm) giving 'the mind/heart is calmed' (literally 'quieted itself') — l'animo = 'the soul/mind.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g137: E = 'and.' Disira is present (poetic) form meaning 'longs for' or 'desires' (verb disirare) and continues the description of what happens in that 'good' — the mind both quiets and longs.
segments.purgatorio-17-g138: Per che is a conjunctive phrase 'wherefore/therefore' introducing the result or consequence. Di before the infinitive giugner is an older Italian usage that marks the infinitive ('to reach/arrive at'). Lui refers back to the object (the good, here rendered as 'him/it'); ciascun is the subject 'each one,' and contende (present indicative of contendere) means 'strives/contends' — 'each strives to reach it.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g139: Se = 'if' (conditional). Lento modifies amore: 'sluggish/slow love' (meaning a love that moves slowly or is misplaced). A lui veder vi tira literally 'pulls you to see him' — a + lui indicates the aim, veder is the infinitive 'to see,' vi is the clitic 'you' (object), and tira (present of tirare) means 'draws/pulls/tends.' O a lui acquistar parallels the first phrase: 'or to acquire him' (acquistar = acquire/gain).
segments.purgatorio-17-g140: Questa = 'this.' Cornice literally means 'frame' but is used figuratively here for one of the 'circles' or rings/terraces of Purgatory — a figurative 'ring' or 'circle' rather than an architectural cornice.
segments.purgatorio-17-g141: Dopo = 'after'; giusto here means 'just/proper' and qualifies penter (an archaic infinitive for 'to repent'). The phrase dopo giusto penter means 'after proper/just repentance.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g142: Ve ne martira is a terse, idiomatic clause: ve (to you/there) + ne ('of it'/'for it') + martira (future or present-subjunctive/poetic form of martirare used here as 'will/may torment'); the construction means 'it will/shall torment you for it' (i.e. will torment you on that account). The clitics ve + ne indicate the indirect/partitive relation to the punishment.
segments.purgatorio-17-g143: Altro ben = 'another/other good' (altro modifies ben). È is the copula 'is.' Che introduces a relative clause 'which'; non fa l'uom felice = 'does not make the man happy' (fare = make). Note the older spelling uom for uomo. The clause defines the preceding 'other good.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g144: Non è felicità is a plain negated identification: 'it is not felicity/happiness.' The verb è remains copular and felicità is the predicate noun.
segments.purgatorio-17-g145: Non è la buona essenza continues the negation: 'it is not the good (or true) essence.' La buona is the article + adjective 'the good,' and essenza = 'essence' in a metaphysical sense.
segments.purgatorio-17-g146: D'ogne = contraction of di + ogne ('of every'). Ben here again means 'good(s)' (the objective goods), frutto e radice = 'fruit and root' — a metaphor: frutto = result/fruit, radice = root/origin. The whole phrase summarizes that this 'other good' is not the fundamental source and result of every good.
segments.purgatorio-17-g76: Noi eravam = subject pronoun + imperfect past of essere (eravamo/eravam variant) used narratively: 'we were'. 'dove più non saliva la scala sù' is a relative/locative clause: 'where the stair no longer rose up.' Note the order più non + verb = 'no longer' (più here = 'any longer'), and 'saliva' is the imperfect of salire (to climb) used by Dante; the final sù reinforces upward direction.
segments.purgatorio-17-g77: ed eravamo affissi: 'and we were affixed/held fast.' Here eravamo is the imperfect indicative of essere and affissi is a past participle used adjectivally to mean 'fixed/stuck/fastened' (stative result), forming a state rather than an action.
segments.purgatorio-17-g78: pur come nave ch’a la piaggia arriva: a simile introduced by 'come' = 'like/as.' 'pur' = 'even/just' (softening/reinforcing the simile). 'ch’a' is the contraction of che + a (relative clause), and 'arriva' is present indicative 'reaches/arrives' used in a vivid simile even though the main narration is past.
segments.purgatorio-17-g79: E io attesi un poco: 'And I waited a little.' 'attesi' is the passato remoto of attendere ('I waited/awaited'), used in narrative. 'un poco' modifies duration ('for a short time'). The explicit subject 'io' adds emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-17-g80: s’io udissi alcuna cosa nel novo girone;: archaic/poetic contraction s’ = se ('if'). 'udissi' is the imperfect subjunctive of udire used here in a potential/conditional sense ('to see whether I might hear'); 'alcuna cosa' = 'anything/some thing'; 'nel novo girone' = 'in the new circle/turn.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g81: poi mi volsi al maestro mio: 'then I turned to my master.' 'volsi' is passato remoto of volgersi/volgere (I turned myself); the reflexive 'mi' marks the verb as turning oneself toward someone. Possessive follows the noun ('maestro mio').
segments.purgatorio-17-g82: e dissi:: simple narrative connector + passato remoto 'dissi' ('and I said'), introducing direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-17-g83: «Dolce mio padre,: vocative address. 'Dolce' = 'dear/sweet' qualifying 'padre'; possessive 'mio' placed between adjective and noun is a normal poetic order for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-17-g84: dì,: imperative second-person singular of dire (or archaic vocative form used as 'tell/say'). It's a direct command/request within the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-17-g85: quale offensione si purga qui nel giro dove semo?: 'what offense is purged here in the circle where we are?' 'si purga' is a passive/impersonal present construction ('is purged/is cleansed'). 'semo' is the archaic/dialectal form of siamo ('we are').
segments.purgatorio-17-g86: Se i piè si stanno,: protasis of a conditional ('If the feet stand/remain'). 'i piè' is the grammatical subject; 'si stanno' is a reflexive or mediopassive use of stare meaning 'stand/are at rest' (present indicative). In Dante the reflexive builds the notion 'are standing still.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g87: non stea tuo sermone»: 'let not your sermon/speech stay.' 'stea' is an archaic present subjunctive form of stare used here to express a negative wish or prohibition ('may...not be' / 'do not let...remain'). 'tuo sermone' = 'your discourse/speech.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g88: Ed elli a me:: narrative formula introducing direct speech. 'E' = and; 'elli' is an archaic form for 'egli' = 'he'; 'a me' marks the indirect object 'to me.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g89: «L’amor del bene,: nominative subject phrase 'The love of the good.' The article + noun + genitive indicate the abstract principle being spoken about; this is the topic of the clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-17-g90: scemo del suo dover,: 'scemo' here means 'diminished' or 'lessened' (an adjectival/past participle use); 'del suo dover' = 'of its duty/obligation'—a genitive phrase specifying how the love has been impaired.
segments.purgatorio-17-g91: quiritta si ristora;: 'quiritta' is an archaic compound/poetic form (apparently 'qui ritto' = 'here straight/upright') meaning 'straightens/sets right'; 'si ristora' is reflexive 'restores itself/refreshes itself.' The clause says the love is restored here to its right condition.
segments.purgatorio-17-g92: qui si ribatte il mal tardato remo.: 'here the belated evil oar is struck back.' 'si ribatte' = reflexive/passive 'is struck back' or 'is repelled'; 'il mal tardato remo' is a metaphorical phrase: 'the evil (mal) whose oar was delayed (tardato)'. The image suggests that the late-returning harm is driven back here.
segments.purgatorio-17-g51: Questo è divino spirito: a straightforward copular clause with the demonstrative subject 'Questo' followed by present indicative 'è' (3rd person singular of essere). 'divino' is a predicative adjective modifying 'spirito' (noun in apposition to 'Questo').
segments.purgatorio-17-g52: 'che' introduces a relative clause modifying 'spirito' ('which'). 'ne la via' is an older construction where 'ne' (= in/within) combines with the article 'la' to mean 'in the way'; 'da ir sù' uses the infinitive 'ir' (to go) plus the adverb 'sù' (up) with 'da' indicating the direction/purpose associated with that way (i.e. 'for going up'); the second clitic 'ne' is resumptive/referring back ('in it' / 'along it'), and 'drizza' is present indicative (3rd sg) of drizzare meaning 'directs/straightens/sets right' (here 'guides'). 'sanza' is the archaic form of 'senza' ('without'), and 'prego' is a noun here meaning 'entreaty/request' (i.e. without asking or entreaty).
segments.purgatorio-17-g53: The clause begins with coordinating 'e' ('and'). 'col' is the contraction of 'con il' ('with the'); 'suo' is a possessive adjective agreeing with 'lume' ('its/whose light'); 'sé medesmo' is an emphatic reflexive ('itself/the very self'), and 'cela' is present indicative (3rd sg) of 'celare' meaning 'hides' — producing the striking image that the spirit conceals itself by means of its own light.
segments.purgatorio-17-g54: 'Sì fa con noi' is idiomatic: 'Sì' = 'thus/so' and 'fa' is impersonal/third-person singular of 'fare' meaning 'it acts/does so'; 'con noi' indicates the people affected ('with us' / 'toward us').
segments.purgatorio-17-g55: 'come l’uom si fa sego' is a simile. 'come' = 'as/like'; 'l’uom' ('l’' = the + 'uom' = man'); 'si fa' is middle/reflexive use of 'fare' meaning 'becomes' or 'is made' (here 'a man becomes'); 'sego' is a noun meaning 'suet' (fat) — the image is of a man made soft/passive like suet.
segments.purgatorio-17-g56: 'ché' = 'because/for'. 'quale' here functions as an indefinite relative ('whoever' / 'such as'); 'aspetta' (3rd sg) = 'awaits/expects'; 'prego' is used as 'entreaty/request' (object of 'expects'); 'e l’uopo vede' = 'and sees the need' where 'l’' is the article before 'uopo' (need) and 'vede' is present indicative 'he sees'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g57: 'malignamente' is an adverb 'maliciously/spitefully'; 'già' here = 'already/by then'; 'si mette' is reflexive 'he sets himself' (3rd sg present) from 'mettere' and implies 'begins/puts himself to'; 'al nego' is an older locution where 'nego' (from negare) denotes 'refusal/denial' — so the clause means 'he already maliciously prepares himself for refusal/negation.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g58: 'Or' = 'now/then'. 'accordiamo' is 1st person plural (let us) of 'accordire' meaning here 'let us grant/comply with'; 'a tanto invito' means 'to such an invitation' (dative of purpose); idiomatically 'accordiamo il piede' = 'let us set foot' or 'let us comply (with our foot = move).' 'il piede' is literal 'the foot' used idiomatically for beginning to move/ascend.
segments.purgatorio-17-g59: 'procacciam' is 1st pl. present/imperative sense 'let us procure/endeavor' (a hortative), followed by the infinitive 'di salir' ('to climb/ascend'). 'pria che' = 'before that/than', and 's’abbui' is the reflexive/impersonal form of 'abbuiare/annuvolarsi' meaning 'it grows dark' (s' + abbui). The clause expresses urgency to ascend before darkness falls.
segments.purgatorio-17-g60: 'ché' = 'because/for'. 'poi' = 'afterwards/then'. 'non si poria' uses an old/flexible modal verb 'poria' (from 'poriare') meaning 'would be possible' (impersonal construction); with 'non' it means 'it would not be possible then.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g61: 'se' = 'if' (conditional). '’l dì' is the contracted article + noun ('the day' = daylight). 'non riede' = 'does not return' (3rd singular present of 'riedere' = to return); the sense is 'if the day does not come back' (i.e. if daylight does not return).
segments.purgatorio-17-g62: 'Così disse il mio duca' uses the passato remoto 'disse' (3rd sing. of dire) meaning 'thus said my guide/duke' — a simple past narration. 'il mio duca' is the subject introduced after the verb (standard word order for reported speech in Italian).
segments.purgatorio-17-g63: 'e io con lui volgemmo i nostri passi ad una scala' contains 'volgemmo' (passato remoto, 1st pl. of volgere) meaning 'we turned/changed direction'; 'i nostri passi' literally 'our steps' (i.e. our course), and 'ad una scala' 'to/toward a stair' — a straightforward narrative sequence.
segments.purgatorio-17-g64: 'e tosto ch’io al primo grado fui,' uses 'tosto' = 'soon/as soon as' and the subordinating 'ch’' (che) introducing a temporal clause. 'io ... fui' is passato remoto of essere ('I was') and 'al primo grado' = 'on the first step/degree' — so: 'and as soon as I was at the first step.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g65: 'senti’mi' is an archaic contraction of 'sentiami' / 'sentii mi' meaning 'I felt' or 'I perceived' (here first-person past sense). 'presso quasi un muover d’ala' = 'near me, as if a motion of a wing' where 'muover' is a noun 'movement' and 'd’ala' 'of wing.' 'ventarmi nel viso' uses the rare/poetic verb 'ventarmare/ventarmi' meaning 'to fan/blow upon me' (the wing's motion caused a breeze), 'e dir' attaches an infinitive designating what was heard/said next.
segments.purgatorio-17-g66: The vocative/exclamatory utterance begins with 'Beati' (plural adjective 'blessed') followed by 'pacifici' (plural adjective 'peaceful' or 'peacemakers/peaceful ones') — a Beatitude-style address.
segments.purgatorio-17-g67: 'che son sanz’ ira mala!' is a relative clause: 'che' = 'who/which'; 'son' = present 'are' (from essere); 'sanz’' is archaic 'senza' = 'without'; 'ira mala' = 'bad/evil anger' (adjective 'mala' agrees with feminine 'ira'). The meaning is 'who are without evil anger' (i.e. free from malicious wrath).
segments.purgatorio-17-g93: Ma = 'but' introducing a contrast. Perché here means 'because/since' (not a question) and introduces the reason clause. Più aperto is an adverbial/participial phrase 'more open/clear' modifying the following verb; intendi is 2nd-person singular present indicative (tu intendi) 'you understand/perceive' (an older, direct address), and ancora 'still/yet' reinforces continuity.
segments.purgatorio-17-g94: Volgi is the 2nd-person singular imperative (turn/redirect) of volgere — a direct command to the addressee. La mente is the definite object 'the mind' and a introduces the indirect object 'to me' (a me). Word order is imperative + object + indirect object.
segments.purgatorio-17-g95: E = 'and' linking to the command. Prenderai is future indicative, 2nd-person singular ('you will take/receive'), so the promise/result follows. Alcun = 'some' (an indefinite quantity) modifies buon frutto, where 'buon' strengthens 'frutto' as 'a beneficial result'. Di nostra dimora = 'from our dwelling/stay' (di = from/of, nostra dimora = our abode/sojourn).
segments.purgatorio-17-g96: Né ... né is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor'. Creator and creatura are the two coordinated nouns. Mai placed at the end intensifies the negation (= 'never').
segments.purgatorio-17-g97: Cominciò is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of cominciare: 'he began'. El is the archaic subject pronoun 'he' placed after the verb by the poet (inversion is common in Dante).
segments.purgatorio-17-g98: Figliuol is the vocative 'son' (address to the listener); the archaic spelling reflects pronunciation of the time and marks direct address.
segments.purgatorio-17-g99: Fu is passato remoto of essere ('was'). Sanza is an archaic preposition meaning 'without'. Amore is the noun 'love' (here the general principle). Together: 'was without love.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g100: O ... o = 'either ... or'. Naturale = 'natural' (innate love). D’ animo = contraction of di + animo = 'of the spirit/mind/soul' (a disambiguating phrase: either natural love or love of the soul).
segments.purgatorio-17-g101: E = 'and'. Tu is the subject pronoun 'you'. ’l is the contracted object pronoun (lo = 'it') placed clitically before sai; sai is present indicative 2nd-person singular of sapere: 'you know it.' (the written apostrophe marks the contraction).
segments.purgatorio-17-g102: Lo naturale = 'the natural (thing)' — a nominalized adjective used as subject. È is present indicative 'is'. Sempre sanza errore = 'always without error' (sanza = archaic 'without').
segments.purgatorio-17-g103: Ma = 'but'. L’altro ('the other') refers back to the other kind of love; puote is an archaic present (can/has power) and is followed by the infinitive errar 'to err'. Per introduces the cause/reason ('because of'). Malo obietto = 'bad object' (a poor aim). O per troppo o per poco di vigore = 'either by too much or by too little strength/force' — three causal alternatives with per repeated.
segments.purgatorio-17-g104: Mentre = 'while/as long as' introducing a temporal clause. Ch’ is the contraction of che = 'that/which'; elli is the archaic pronoun referring back to love (here used impersonally), and è nel primo ben diretto = 'is directed toward the first/foremost good' (diretto is a past participle/adjectival use meaning 'directed').
segments.purgatorio-17-g105: E = 'and'. Ne’ secondi = contraction (in + i) 'in the secondary ones' (i secondi = the secondary goods). Sé stesso misura = 'measures itself / keeps itself within measure' — sé stesso is reflexive and misura here functions as 'measures' (3rd-person present) meaning 'moderates itself.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g106: Esser (infinitive) together with non può (3rd-person present of potere) forms an impersonal construction: 'it cannot be the cause...' Cagion di mal diletto = 'cause of bad/ill pleasure' (i.e. cause of harmful delight). Word order (infinitive before the modal) is a poetic/archaic option.
segments.purgatorio-17-g107: Ma quando = 'but when'. Al mal = 'to/against evil'. Si torce is reflexive present of torcere: 'it turns itself' or 'is bent' toward evil; this marks a change of orientation. (Torce is a present-form verb here, reflexive.)
segments.purgatorio-17-g108: O con più cura o con men che non dee = 'either with too much care or with less than it ought' (men che non dee is an idiomatic comparative: 'less than it should [be]'). Corre nel bene = literally 'runs in/to the good' (corre = 3rd-person present of correre), here meaning it moves/operates with respect to the good — the phrase indicates misdirected activity.
segments.purgatorio-17-g109: Contra = 'against'. ’L fattore = 'the Maker' (the Creator). Adovra is the present (3rd-person) of adovrare/adoperare 'uses/applies'; sua fattura = 'his own workmanship/creation'. The clause means that love, when misapplied, turns the creature's own handiwork against its Maker.
segments.purgatorio-17-g110: Quinci = 'from this' or 'hence'. Comprender puoi is an inversion typical of older/poetic Italian: the infinitive comprender (to understand) precedes the finite verb puoi (you can) and together mean 'you can understand'. Ch’esser convene is a subordinate impersonal clause introduced by ch’ (that) with the infinitive esser (to be) and the impersonal verb convene (archaic form of conviene, 'is fitting/necessary'), so: 'that it is fitting that...'. The remainder gives the predicate of that clause: amor (subject) + sementa (here used as a substantive 'seed' in poetic diction) + in voi 'in you' + d’ogne virtute e d’ogne operazion che merta pene 'of every virtue and of every action that deserves punishment'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g111: Or = 'now' / 'then', a discourse particle introducing the next argument or consequence.
segments.purgatorio-17-g112: Perché here = 'because'. Mai in a negative context gives the sense 'ever' (i.e. 'can never'). Non può = 'cannot'. Da la salute = 'from the salvation' (da + noun). Amor del suo subietto volger viso is literal 'love cannot turn its face from the salvation of its own subject' — volger viso = 'turn (its) face/gaze' and subietto = 'the subject (person) loved'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g113: Da l’odio proprio son le cose tute is inverted word order: literally 'from proper hatred are all the things' → 'all things come from (their) proper hatred' or 'are governed by their own hatred'. Son = are (present), tute = archaic form meaning 'all/every'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g114: E perché = 'and because'. Intender non si può diviso uses intender (infinitive 'to understand') with the impersonal/reflexive si: 'cannot be understood separately' — diviso is a past participle/adjectival use meaning 'separately' or 'apart'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g115: E per sé stante = 'and standing by itself' / 'and as existing on its own' (per sé stante is a fixed phrase meaning 'independently'). Stante is a present participle/adjectival form 'standing/being'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g116: Alcuno esser dal primo = 'that anything (alcuno) could be from the first' — esser is the infinitive 'to be' and dal primo = 'from the first' (the primary cause). This continues the remark that nothing stands apart from the first cause.
segments.purgatorio-17-g117: Da quello odiare ogne effetto è deciso is a result clause: da quello = 'because of that' or 'from that (cause)'; odiare ogne effetto is the infinitive phrase 'to hate every effect/outcome'; è deciso = 'is determined/decided' (deciso used impersonally). So: 'for that reason it is decided that every effect should be hated.'
segments.purgatorio-17-g118: Resta = 'it remains' or 'what remains' — the formula introducing a concluding point after the preceding reasoning.
segments.purgatorio-17-g119: Se dividendo bene stimo is a parenthetical concessive: se = 'if' (here the sense 'if' is not among the supplied senses list, so marked separately), dividendo bene = gerund phrase 'by dividing/separating well' and stimo = 'I judge/esteem' (first person present). Together: 'if, by dividing properly, I judge'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g120: Che ’l mal che s’ama è del prossimo = 'that the evil which is loved is of/comes from the neighbor' (i.e. the evil one loves concerns one's neighbor). The relative clause che s’ama uses the reflexive/impersonal si (s’) meaning 'is loved' or 'one loves'; è del prossimo = 'belongs to the neighbor' or 'is that of one's neighbor'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g121: Ed esso amor nasce in tre modi in vostro limo = 'and that same love is born in three ways in your slime' (vostro limo = 'your muddy/clayey nature' = 'your earthly disposition'). Nasce is present 'is born/arises', modi = 'ways/manners'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g122: È chi = 'there is one who' / 'there are those who' — a common Dantean formula introducing examples of people.
segments.purgatorio-17-g123: Per esser suo vicin soppresso = 'because his neighbor has been suppressed/removed' (per + gerund/infinitive phrase expresses the cause: 'for the being of his neighbor suppressed'). Vicin = 'neighbor' (near person) and soppresso is a past participle 'suppressed/put down/killed' describing that condition.
segments.purgatorio-17-g124: Spera eccellenza = 'he hopes for advancement/excellence' — spera (verb 'hopes') takes the noun eccellenza (high rank, advancement) as its object.
segments.purgatorio-17-g125: E sol per questo brama ch’el sia di sua grandezza in basso messo = 'and only for this he longs that he be put low from his greatness'. Sol (here 'only') is poetic/archaic; brama functions as 'desire/longing' and the clause ch’el sia... uses the subjunctive sia to express the wished-for state 'that he be put low'. In basso messo = 'placed low' (messo as past participle).
segments.purgatorio-17-g126: È chi podere, ... = 'There are some who [because of] power, ...' — podere is a noun here meaning 'power/strength/authority', introducing the next list of things the person fears to lose.
segments.purgatorio-17-g127: Grazia = 'favor' or 'grace' — here one item in a series of goods (power, favor, honor, fame). It is a noun object of the implicit fear of loss.
segments.purgatorio-17-g128: Onore e fama teme di perder perch’ altri sormonti = 'he fears to lose honor and fame because another may overtake/surpass him.' Teme di perder = 'fears to lose'; perch’ = 'because'; sormonti is subjunctive/potential 'may surmount/overtake'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g129: Onde s’attrista sì che ’l contrario ama = 'wherefore/whence he grows sad so that he loves the contrary.' Onde (here 'wherefore') introduces the consequence; s’attrista is reflexive 'becomes sad', sì che introduces the result 'so that', and 'il contrario ama' = 'he loves the opposite/contrary'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g130: Ed è chi per ingiuria par ch’aonti = 'And there are those who, because of an injury/insult, seem (par) that ...' (aonti is a problematic/manuscript variant). Per ingiuria = 'through/by reason of an injury/insult'; par = 'seems'; ch’aonti is a clause with aonti (an uncertain or corrupt word in manuscripts — likely related to 'onta' = 'shame' or a verbal form), so the line means roughly 'seems ashamed' or 'seems to be afflicted'.
segments.purgatorio-17-g131: Sì che si fa de la vendetta ghiotto = 'so that he becomes greedy for vengeance.' Sì che = 'so that'; si fa (reflexive use of fare) = 'becomes' (fa here has the sense 'become'); de la vendetta = 'for/from vengeance'; ghiotto = 'greedy/rapturous for' (an adjective meaning eager or gluttonous for).
segments.purgatorio-17-g132: E tal convien che ’l male altrui impronti = 'And such it is necessary that he imprint the other's evil.' Convien is impersonal 'it is necessary/it behooves'; the clause after che takes the subjunctive/imperfective sense (impronti is subjunctive/optative here) meaning 'that he stamp/transfer his harm onto others' (altrui = 'of others').

sentences.purgatorio-17-s18: By then the last rays that follow the night had risen so high above us that the stars were appearing from many directions.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s19: O my power, why do you vanish so?
sentences.purgatorio-17-s20: '', I said to myself, for I felt the strength of my legs put into a truce.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s9: I am she who mourns, mother, for you before I mourn another’s ruin.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s10: Just as sleep is broken where, all at once, a new light strikes the closed face — which, rent, flashes before it wholly dies — so my imagination fell down as soon as light struck my face, far more strongly than is our custom.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s11: I turned around to see where I was, when a voice said, “Here one climbs,” which drew me away from every other intention; and it made my desire so ready to look at who was speaking that it never rests unless it is brought face to face (with its object).
sentences.purgatorio-17-s12: But just as the sun burdens our sight and, through its excess, veils it with its brightness, so my strength there was lacking.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s37: The love that surrenders itself too much to it laments above us in three circles; but, since it is treated as threefold, I will hold my peace, so that you may seek it for yourself.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s1: Remember, reader, if ever on the mountain a fog took you so that you could see no more than through the skin of a mole; how, when the damp and thick vapors begin to thin, the sphere of the sun feebly enters through them; and your light imagination will be quick to come to see how I again saw the sun earlier, which was already going down.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s2: So, matching my steps to the faithful steps of my master, I came out of that cloud into the sun's waning rays on the low shores.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s3: O imaginative faculty that so often steals outward from us, so that a man does not notice (it) because a thousand trumpets sound all around — what moves you if sense does not provide you?
sentences.purgatorio-17-s4: Move yourself, light, that is formed in heaven, whether by itself or by the will that down here perceives it.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s5: From her impiety — who changed shape into the bird that most delights in singing — the trace appeared in my imagination; and here my mind was so narrowed within itself that nothing from outside came that was then received by her.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s6: Then a crucifix fell into my lofty imagination, spiteful and fierce in its appearance, and so it seemed to die away; around it stood the great Ahasuerus, Esther his wife, and the righteous Mordecai, who was so upright in word and deed.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s7: And as this image broke apart of its own accord, like a bubble missing the water beneath which it was made, there rose in my vision a maiden weeping loudly, and she was saying: 'O queen, why through anger have you wished to be nothing?'
sentences.purgatorio-17-s8: You have cut yourself (or been cut) so as not to lose Lavinia; now you have lost me!
sentences.purgatorio-17-s33: This three‑fold love is lamented down here; now I want you to understand the other, which runs toward the good with a corrupted order.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s34: Each one confusedly seizes upon some good in which the soul is calmed and longs; therefore each strives to reach it.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s35: If slow love draws you to see him or to gain him, this ring, after fitting repentance, will torment you for it.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s36: Another 'good' is that which does not make a man happy; it is not felicity, not the good essence, the fruit and root of every good.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s21: We were where the stair no longer climbed upward, and we were fixed fast, just like a ship that reaches the shore.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s22: And I waited a little, to see if I might hear anything in the new circle; then I turned to my master and said: 'My sweet father, tell me, what offense is purged here in the circle where we are?'
sentences.purgatorio-17-s23: If the feet remain standing, let not your speech remain.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s24: And he to me: 'The love of good, diminished from its proper duty, here straightens and is restored; here the ill, the belated oar, is struck back.'
sentences.purgatorio-17-s13: “This is a divine spirit, which guides us along the way up without being asked, and with its light hides itself.”
sentences.purgatorio-17-s14: Thus it acts with us, as a man becomes suet; for whoever expects entreaty and sees the need already sets himself spitefully to refusal.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s15: Now let us yield our foot to such an invitation; let us contrive to climb before it grows dark, for afterwards it would not be possible, if the day does not return.”
sentences.purgatorio-17-s16: Thus spoke my duke, and I with him turned our steps toward a stair; and as soon as I was on the first step, I felt near me, as it were, the movement of a wing, and it fanned my face and (I heard it) say: 'Blessed are the peaceful, who are without evil anger!'
sentences.purgatorio-17-s25: But because you still understand it more clearly, turn your mind to me, and you will gain some good fruit from our dwelling.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s26: “Neither creator nor creature ever,” he began, “son, was without love, either natural or of the soul; and you know it.”
sentences.purgatorio-17-s27: The natural (love) is always without error, but the other can err because of a bad object or because of too much or too little force.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s28: While it is rightly directed toward the first good, and in the secondary ones measures itself, it cannot be the cause of ill-pleasure; but when it turns to evil, or with too much care or with less than it ought, it runs into the good, it employs its own workmanship against the Maker.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s29: From this you can understand that it is fitting that love be the seed in you of every virtue and of every action that merits punishment.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s30: Now, because love can never turn its face away from the salvation of its own subject, all things are from proper hatred; and since things cannot be understood separately or as standing by themselves, it is decided that every effect is to be hated on that account.
sentences.purgatorio-17-s31: What remains, if I judge the distinctions rightly, is that the evil which is loved belongs to the neighbor; and that same love is born in three ways in your slime (your earthly nature).
sentences.purgatorio-17-s32: There are those who, because their neighbor has been removed, hope for advancement and, for that alone, long that he be cast down from his greatness; there are those who fear losing power, favor, honor and fame lest another overtake them, wherefore they grow so saddened that they love the contrary; and there are those who, through an injury, seem shamed, so that they become greedy for vengeance, and it is fitting that they imprint their harm on others.
